case.name: Alex
case.history: The patient has a history of substance abuse and has been through rehab to overcome it. He has had issues with his family, particularly with his mother, where he has felt rejected and emotionally neglected. He has struggled with obesity since childhood, which has affected his self-esteem and body image. He also has a history of being victimized and bullied due to his weight.
case.intermediate_belief:
Helpless: I'm just not very good at handling stress and I have poor self-control, which is why I need to not put myself in stressful situations. 
 Unlovable: If I show my true self, people will reject me the way my mother rejected me.
case.intermediate_belief_depression:
Helpless: There's nothing I can do to change my situation. I cannot control myself. 
Unlovable: I don't deserve to be happy, so why even try to stay clean?
case.coping_strategies: The patient has adopted avoidance as a coping strategy by distancing himself from his family to reduce exposure to negativity and conflict. He has started scheduling pleasant activities and planning his day ahead of time in order to maintain a sense of control over his emotions and circumstances.
case.situation: Alex's cousin invited him to attend his upcoming wedding.
case.auto_thought: It will be stressful and negative; people will ask me questions I cannot answer or don't want to answer; my mum would be ashamed of me and critical of me as always; They don't want me there anyway, nobody likes me in this family.
case.behavior: Ignored the invitation and did not respond to the RSVP request. Ignored phone calls from family.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Hi there! I'm glad you're here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I'm not really sure what to focus on. There are so many things on my mind lately, and it feels overwhelming. Maybe I should just talk about how stressed I’ve been feeling? But I'm not sure if that's what you mean.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Therapist: It sounds like you have a lot on your mind, and the feeling of being overwhelmed is really common when there’s so much going on. Stress can really take a toll. Can you tell me a bit more about what has been causing you to feel stressed lately?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I'm really not sure where to start. I guess I’ve been feeling stressed because of work and personal responsibilities piling up. It just feels like I can never catch a break, and I often worry that I’m not doing enough. I think that might be part of what’s making me feel overwhelmed.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Therapist: It sounds like both work and personal responsibilities are weighing heavily on you, and that sense of not doing enough is contributing to your stress. That must be really tough to manage. When you think about your responsibilities, what specific thoughts or worries come up for you?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I guess when I think about my responsibilities, I worry that I'm not meeting the expectations I have for myself or what others expect from me. It feels like no matter what I do, it’s never enough, and that really adds to my stress. I often think I should be more productive or handle everything better, but then I just end up feeling overwhelmed and anxious.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It seems like there's a lot of pressure you're putting on yourself to meet expectations, both your own and those of others. That feeling of never being enough can be really heavy. When you think about those expectations, what do you feel inside?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I'm not really sure what I feel inside when I think about those expectations. I guess it's a mix of frustration and anxiety. Sometimes I feel like I'm letting myself down, and that just adds to the stress. I want to be able to handle everything, but it feels like I'm always falling short. It's hard to shake off that feeling of not measuring up.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like you're experiencing a complex mix of frustration and anxiety when it comes to those expectations. Feeling like you're letting yourself down can be really disheartening, especially when you want to manage everything effectively. Can you share more about what those expectations look like for you? What do you think you need to achieve or how do you believe you should be handling things?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I'm not sure how to describe my expectations. I think they come from a mix of things, like my upbringing and what I see others achieving. I feel like I should be able to balance everything perfectly and always be productive. It’s like there's this voice in my head telling me that if I’m not achieving certain goals or meeting certain standards, then I’m failing. But I don't really know what those specific goals should be. It just feels like a constant pressure to do better, and when I don’t meet that pressure, it makes me feel even more overwhelmed and anxious.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like those expectations are deeply rooted in your upbringing and influenced by what you see around you, creating a persistent pressure to achieve and to be productive. The voice in your head that defines failure seems to add to your sense of overwhelm. It must be challenging to navigate this when the goals aren’t clearly defined. When you reflect on that voice and its demands, what do you think it’s really trying to communicate to you?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I'm not really sure what that voice is trying to communicate. Sometimes it feels like it's just pushing me to do more and be better, but I think it also stems from a fear of disappointing myself and others. I guess it’s like it wants me to strive for something, but I don’t even know what that something is. It just creates this constant feeling of pressure. I wish I could quiet that voice and find a way to feel okay with where I am instead of always chasing some undefined standard.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It seems like that voice represents a mix of motivation and fear, pushing you to strive for something you can't quite define. The desire to quiet it and feel okay with where you are is understandable, especially when it feels like a constant source of pressure. Can you describe a time when you felt more at ease with yourself, even in the midst of your responsibilities? What was different for you then?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: That's a tough question. I can't really think of a specific time when I felt completely at ease. There are moments when I feel a bit more relaxed, like when I'm spending time with friends or doing something I enjoy, but even then, there's always this nagging thought in the back of my mind about what I should be doing instead. I guess maybe those moments are fleeting, and the pressure always seems to creep back in. I wish I could hold onto that feeling of ease for longer, but it feels like it's just out of reach most of the time.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like those moments of relaxation and enjoyment are brief but meaningful for you, yet the pressure always seems to return, making it hard to fully embrace that ease. It must be frustrating to feel like it's just out of reach. When you spend time with friends or engage in activities you enjoy, what do you notice about your thoughts and feelings during those moments? How do they differ from how you feel when you're focused on your responsibilities?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I guess when I'm with friends, I tend to be a bit lighter and more carefree. My thoughts are less about what I should be doing and more about enjoying that moment. I feel like I can laugh and just be myself without the constant pressure. But even in those moments, there's still this underlying worry that I should be doing something more productive. It's like I can't fully let go of that pressure, no matter how hard I try. I wish I could experience that sense of ease more often, but it feels like I'm always battling with my own expectations.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like being with friends allows you to connect with a lighter, more carefree side of yourself, where you can enjoy the moment. Yet, even in those joyful times, the pressure to be productive lingers in the background, making it difficult to fully embrace the experience. That must be exhausting. When you think about those moments of ease and the pressure that follows, what do you think it would feel like to allow yourself to set those expectations aside, even just for a little while?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I think it would feel really freeing to set those expectations aside, even just for a little while. It would be nice to not feel that pressure constantly weighing on me. But at the same time, I worry about what would happen if I did that. Would I still be able to get things done? Would I feel guilty for not being productive? It's hard to imagine what that would look like, and I guess there's a part of me that fears letting go, even if just momentarily.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like the idea of letting go of those expectations feels both freeing and frightening for you. The worry about what might happen if you do is understandable, especially when you’re so accustomed to that pressure. What do you think it is about letting go that feels so challenging for you?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I think letting go feels challenging because I've tied so much of my self-worth to being productive and meeting those expectations. If I were to step back and not do what I feel I should be doing, I worry it might mean I'm not living up to who I think I should be. There's this fear that if I let go, I might disappoint myself or others, and that thought alone makes it hard to even consider. It’s like I'm stuck in this cycle of pressure, and imagining a break from it feels both appealing and terrifying at the same time.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It seems like your sense of self-worth is closely linked to productivity and meeting those expectations, which makes the idea of letting go feel so daunting. The fear of disappointing yourself or others adds another layer of complexity to it. That cycle of pressure sounds really difficult to navigate. When you think about that connection between your self-worth and productivity, what do you believe it says about how you view yourself?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I think it says that I place a lot of pressure on myself to achieve and be productive in order to feel valuable. It's like I've internalized this belief that my worth is determined by what I accomplish, and that makes it hard to see myself as enough just as I am. I often feel like I need to prove something, not just to others but to myself. It's exhausting, and I wish I could find a way to separate my self-worth from my productivity, but it feels so ingrained in how I see myself.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like you've developed a strong belief that your value is tied to your accomplishments, which creates a heavy burden to carry. The desire to prove your worth to yourself and others can be really exhausting. It must be challenging to feel like you have to constantly meet those standards to be seen as enough. When you think about separating your self-worth from productivity, what do you hope that would look like for you? What would it feel like to embrace your worth without those conditions?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I think if I could separate my self-worth from my productivity, it would feel like a huge weight has been lifted off my shoulders. I imagine I would be able to appreciate myself for who I am, rather than what I achieve. It would mean allowing myself to just exist without that constant pressure to prove my value. I guess it would feel more peaceful, but at the same time, I’m scared of what that might mean for my motivation. I worry that if I don’t have that pressure, I might not push myself to accomplish anything at all. It’s a confusing thought.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like the idea of separating your self-worth from your productivity brings up a mix of hope and fear for you. The thought of feeling more peaceful and appreciating yourself for who you are is very appealing, yet there's concern that it might impact your motivation. It’s understandable to feel conflicted about this. Can you explore a bit more about what motivation means to you? What does it look like when you feel motivated, and how does that connect with your sense of self?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I think motivation for me often feels tied to that pressure to achieve. When I feel motivated, it's usually because I have a clear goal in mind or there's something I feel I need to prove. It pushes me to get things done, but it also feels really intense and stressful. I guess I associate being motivated with that sense of urgency and anxiety, which makes it hard to separate it from my self-worth. I’m not sure what it would look like to feel motivated without that pressure. It’s confusing because I want to feel driven, but I also want to find a way to feel okay with just being myself.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like motivation for you is deeply intertwined with that sense of urgency and the pressure to achieve, which adds stress to the process. The desire to feel driven while also wanting to be okay with just being yourself creates a real tension. When you think about what drives your motivation, aside from that pressure, are there aspects of it that you enjoy or find fulfilling? What might that look like for you?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I guess when I think about what drives my motivation, it’s hard to separate it from that pressure to achieve. I feel like I’ve always been taught to work hard and strive for success, so it’s become this intense part of my identity. Sometimes I do find fulfillment in accomplishing tasks, but more often than not, it feels like it’s just a means to prove something. I wish I could experience motivation in a way that feels more enjoyable rather than stressful. It’s just tough to imagine what that would look like for me.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like your motivation is deeply rooted in your upbringing and tied to your identity, making it difficult to separate from that pressure to achieve. While you do find some fulfillment in accomplishing tasks, it often feels more like a way to prove something than a source of joy. That must be really challenging. If you were to envision a different kind of motivation—one that feels enjoyable and less stressful—what kind of activities or goals do you think might foster that feeling for you?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I think envisioning a different kind of motivation is really tough for me. I just can’t seem to shake the idea that my worth is tied to what I achieve. I guess if I were to imagine it, maybe it would involve doing things simply because I enjoy them or find them interesting, rather than feeling like I have to prove something. But even that feels hard to picture because I’m so used to that pressure. It's like I’m in this cycle where I can’t see beyond the need to be productive. I wish I could break free from that mindset, but it feels daunting.

turns.therapist: Hi there! I'm glad you're here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?
turns.client: I'm not really sure what to focus on. There are so many things on my mind lately, and it feels overwhelming. Maybe I should just talk about how stressed I’ve been feeling? But I'm not sure if that's what you mean.
